summary: McKenzie outlines his development timetable and budget for his next software project (which would become Kalzumeus), planned alongside his day job. He commits to building a Rails application in 20 hours per week with an April 1st alpha target and summer launch. He details plans to develop semi-publicly, sharing market analysis, specs, and sales figures while keeping the project name private. The total initial budget is estimated at $1,500-$2,000, with monthly costs kept low enough to reach profitability quickly.
key_points: Plans to build a Rails app in 20 hours/week alongside his day job, with disciplined time tracking
Will develop semi-publicly—sharing market, spec, and sales figures but not source code or project name
Strategic sequencing: build the app first, then bring in outside experts (designer, possibly lawyer) to turn it into a business
Total budget of $1,500-$2,000 covering hosting, design, legal, and LLC filing costs
Self-aware observation that growth strategy is the most important part yet has received the least planning
money_quotes: I've drunken deeply from the Rails koolaid and am now afflicted with an acute condition known as programming fever.
I'm decided on developing this semi-publicly — I'll be publishing the market, the spec, the thinking behind it, pretty much everything but the source code.
The little bureaucratic hoops you have to jump through to make money are so annoying.
If I hire a lawyer, he is going to be put on a strict $500 leash.
Grow business. This is the most important part and so naturally I have given it the smallest section of this post and have not done much writing in my design notebook about it. D'oh.
I expect revenue growth to be a significant multiple of Bingo Card Creator's.
key_insight: A bootstrapped software business can be launched on a shoestring budget of under $2,000 by building the product yourself, sequencing expenses carefully, and bringing in outside help only after the core application is proven.
